text: Hello friends. This is a case of total laparoscopic hysterectomy. This patient has DUB and she has the previous couple of caesarean section. So it was planned to do the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper to coagulate the right upper pedicles, including the round ligament located four centimeters lateral, demonstrating the effectiveness of bipolar instruments in laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon completes the coagulation of the round ligament using a bipolar device, ensuring the tissue color remains brown to avoid overcooking. The fallopian tube is also coagulated, with visible bubbling indicating proper hemostasis.

The surgeon coagulates the fallopian tube approximately three centimeters lateral using successive desiccation. Following coagulation, scissors are used for dissection. The major salpinx on the right side is visibly separated during the procedure.
The external iliac artery on the right side is visibly pulsating within the triangle of dome. The mesosalpinx has been dissected, and the surgeon prepares to cut it.

The surgeon dissects the mesosalpinx and identifies the ovarian ligament, positioning closer to the ovary to minimize bleeding. The major ovarian vessels are coagulated using electrosurgical techniques.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesovarian and proceeds to separate the broad ligament, extending the dissection up to 2.5 centimeters without separating the peritoneum.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right upper pedicle and proceeds to the left side using a ligature device. The round ligament on the left side is being coagulated with the ligature.

The surgeon uses a cutting blade to efficiently cut the infundibulopelvic ligament, as the ovary on this side is planned for removal. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is then opened, reducing instrumentation time by alternating between bipolar and scissors for dissection.

The surgeon pushes the uterus towards the five o'clock position and begins separating the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The assistant maintains the uterus in a retroverted position while the surgeon uses the left hand to stretch and separate the uterus from the five to seven o'clock positions, reaching the midline before continuing to the opposite side.

The surgeon continues separating the peritoneum at the seven o'clock position, encountering adhesions with the bladder tissue. Blunt dissection is used to separate these adhesions, and if necessary, the tissue is lateralized from lateral to medial. A window is created using a harmonic device to aid in separation.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the bladder, revealing the vaginal fascia with a criss-cross pattern of vessels that are clearly visible and should be carefully separated.

The vaginal fascia is clearly visible, and the posterior peritoneum is being separated. The uterus is positioned at two o'clock for the left side and ten o'clock for the right side of the posterior peritoneum. The surgeon uses a ligature to coagulate the ascending uterine vessels before proceeding to the uterine vessels.

The surgeon coagulates the right uterine artery without cutting it, then moves to the left uterine artery, coagulating it initially. The left uterine artery is coagulated again and finally cut, completing the process on the left side.

The surgeon controls minor venous bleeding using a ligature and continues the dissection. The uterine artery is visible medially, while the junction of the cervical vaginal fascia is positioned laterally. The left side dissection is completed, with the Macandrot partially cut.
The surgeon performs final coagulation and cutting near the cervix on the right side, securing the uterine artery. The uterine artery is visible, and the surgeon prepares to start the colpotomy.

The surgeon secures the bladder and uses a full circle colpotomizer to cut the vagina, employing a harmonic instrument for precision. The upper and posterior sections of the colpotomy are completed, followed by the right side using the harmonic tip. The white colpotomizer is visible, and the uterus is fully separated, ready to be pulled through the vagina for removal.
The surgeon prepares to remove the uterus through the vagina, occasionally bisecting it if necessary. Once the uterus is extracted, the vaginal opening is sutured.

The surgeon uses a fully curved needle to perform continuous suturing at the corner of the uterosacral ligament. The needle is held with stone holding forceps and passed through the vagina, with gloves and sponge packing to prevent gas leakage. The suturing progresses from right to left, terminating with a surgeon's knot using number one vicryl, assisted by an assistant to ensure the knot is secure.

The surgeon completes the final wrap of the surgeon's knot and cuts the suture, safely removing it through a 5mm port. The surgical area is thoroughly irrigated and suctioned to check for any bleeding, revealing clear fluid without any signs of bleeding or omental tissue. The procedure concludes with a final inspection of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs repeated lavage to ensure there is no bleeding, followed by final irrigation and suctioning of any remaining blood. The surgical field appears clear and free of bleeding.
